findings.description: Command injection (CWE-78): attacker-controlled job.payload source/dest are interpolated into an f-string and run with subprocess shell=True, allowing arbitrary RCE via shell metacharacters (e.g. source='x; rm -rf /').

Path traversal / file-disclosure & SSRF sink (CWE-22/CWE-918): src and dest are passed unvalidated to ImageMagick convert, so even after removing shell=True an attacker can read/write arbitrary paths or trigger remote fetches; whitelist/validate paths.
Missing input validation (CWE-20): job.payload values (source/dest/values) are consumed with no schema, type, or bounds checks, which is the root enabler of the injection and traversal sinks above.
Log injection / unbounded log volume (CWE-117): full attacker-controlled payload is logged; json.dumps neutralizes CRLF forging but oversized payloads enable log-volume/cost DoS.

Untrusted input to sum() (CWE-400, weak): attacker-controlled values list could carry huge ints (CPU/memory) or non-numeric entries (TypeError); mostly a robustness/logic concern, deferred to sibling critics.
Retry loop resets attempt=0 each iteration so attempt<MAX_RETRIES is always true; a permanently-failing job loops forever (availability/DoS shape) — root cause is logic, deferred to logic critic.
Blocking time.sleep called inside async poll_loop stalls the event loop (should be await asyncio.sleep); not security, noted for sibling critics.
